questions.question:
Let S be a set of n elements. The number of ordered pairs in the largest and the smallest equivalence relations on S are:
A. n and n
B. n^2 and n
C. n^2 and 0
D. n and 1
questions.to_find: Number of ordered pairs in the largest and smallest equivalence relations on an n-element set

understand.plain: An equivalence relation must be reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. The largest equivalence relation is S x S (all n^2 pairs), and the smallest is the identity relation (just the n diagonal pairs {(a,a) : a in S}). Both S x S and the identity relation are valid equivalence relations on any set.

keywords.explain: S x S contains all n^2 ordered pairs; it is reflexive (trivially), symmetric (if (a,b) in S x S then (b,a) in S x S), and transitive. This is the coarsest partition: one equivalence class containing all elements.
The identity/diagonal relation I = {(a,a) : a in S} has exactly n pairs. It is reflexive, symmetric (only self-pairs), and transitive. This is the finest partition: each element in its own class.
keywords.example: For S = {1,2}: I = {(1,1),(2,2)}, exactly 2 = n pairs.

keywords.explain: A relation that is simultaneously reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. It partitions the set into equivalence classes.
keywords.example: Congruence mod k on integers is an equivalence relation.

understand.visual_alt: The equivalence relations on S range from the finest (identity, n pairs on the diagonal) to the coarsest (S x S, n^2 pairs, one class for all elements).

given.aim: Find the sizes (number of ordered pairs) of the largest and smallest equivalence relations on an n-element set S.

terms.meaning: A set with n elements
terms.connects: Equivalence relations are subsets of S x S

terms.meaning: The set of all n^2 ordered pairs (a,b) with a,b in S
terms.example: For n=2, S x S has 4 pairs
terms.connects: Largest equivalence relation = S x S

terms.meaning: The set {(a,a) : a in S} of n diagonal pairs
terms.connects: Smallest equivalence relation = I_S

terms.meaning: The subset of all elements related to a given element
terms.example: Under I_S each class is a singleton; under S x S the whole S is one class
terms.connects: Partition structure determines the size of the equivalence relation

given.plan: Recall that equivalence relations correspond to partitions. The coarsest partition (one block = all of S) gives S x S with n^2 pairs; the finest partition (n singleton blocks) gives the diagonal with n pairs.

steps.title: Largest equivalence relation
steps.formula_raw: R is an equivalence relation iff R is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive
steps.apply: S x S contains all n^2 ordered pairs. Check: every (a,a) is in S x S (reflexive); (a,b) in S x S implies (b,a) in S x S (symmetric); transitivity holds trivially. So S x S is a valid equivalence relation with n^2 pairs.
steps.note: The corresponding partition has exactly one equivalence class: the entire set S.

steps.title: Smallest equivalence relation
steps.formula_raw: R is an equivalence relation iff R is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive
steps.apply: The identity relation I_S = {(a,a) : a in S} has exactly n pairs. It is reflexive by definition; it is symmetric because the only pairs are self-pairs; it is transitive because (a,a) and (a,a) gives (a,a). Any equivalence relation must at least contain all (a,a) by reflexivity, so I_S is the minimum with n pairs.
steps.note: The corresponding partition has n singleton equivalence classes.

solution.result: Largest: n^2 pairs (= S x S); Smallest: n pairs (= identity relation). Answer: B. n^2 and n.

questions.formula_note: The equivalence-relation axiom (reflexive + symmetric + transitive) directly determines both bounds: reflexivity forces all n diagonal pairs (lower bound n), and the coarsest single-class partition gives n^2 (upper bound).

questions.question:
Consider the binary relation R = {(x,y),(y,x),(z,z)} on the set {x,y,z}. Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. R is symmetric but NOT antisymmetric
B. R is NOT symmetric but antisymmetric
C. R is both symmetric and antisymmetric
D. R is neither symmetric nor antisymmetric
questions.answer: A. R is symmetric but NOT antisymmetric
questions.to_find: Whether the given binary relation is symmetric, antisymmetric, both, or neither

understand.plain: To test symmetry, check that whenever (a,b) is in R, (b,a) is also in R. To test antisymmetry, check that whenever both (a,b) and (b,a) are in R, we must have a = b. Here R has (x,y) and (y,x) together, which tests both properties at once, and (z,z) is a neutral self-pair.

keywords.explain: For all a,b: if (a,b) in R then (b,a) in R. Every pair has its reverse also in R.
For all a,b: if (a,b) in R and (b,a) in R then a = b. No two distinct elements are mutually related.
keywords.example: R = {(1,2),(2,3)} is antisymmetric (no mutual pairs at all).

understand.visual_alt: The relation R contains the mutual pair (x,y)/(y,x) and the self-loop (z,z). The missing pairs (x,z),(z,x),(y,z),(z,y),(x,x),(y,y) break symmetry, while the mutual pair (x,y)/(y,x) with x != y breaks antisymmetry.

given.aim: Determine symmetry and antisymmetry properties of R = {(x,y),(y,x),(z,z)} on {x,y,z}.

terms.meaning: The relation {(x,y),(y,x),(z,z)} on the 3-element set {x,y,z}
terms.example: x is related to y and y to x; z is related to itself
terms.connects: Need to check all pairs for symmetry and antisymmetry

terms.meaning: For each (a,b) in R, verify (b,a) is also in R
terms.example: (x,y) in R but (y,x) is also in R. (y,x) in R and (x,y) in R. (z,z) in R and (z,z) in R. But we need ALL pairs present; (x,x) is NOT in R, so R is not reflexive either.
terms.connects: Actually the symmetry check only looks at what IS in R, not what is absent.

terms.meaning: If (a,b) in R AND (b,a) in R then a must equal b
terms.example: (x,y) in R and (y,x) in R but x != y, so antisymmetry FAILS
terms.connects: The mutual pair (x,y)/(y,x) is the counterexample to antisymmetry

terms.meaning: Find (a,b) in R such that (b,a) is NOT in R
terms.example: Is (x,z) or (z,x) needed? (x,z) is NOT in R, but also (z,x) is NOT in R. What matters is: are there pairs in R whose reverse is missing? (x,y) in R and (y,x) in R - both present. (z,z) self-pair - fine. So WAIT: is R actually symmetric?
terms.connects: Let me re-check: R = {(x,y),(y,x),(z,z)}. For symmetry: (x,y)->check (y,x) in R: YES. (y,x)->check (x,y) in R: YES. (z,z)->check (z,z): YES. So R IS symmetric for the pairs that ARE in R!

given.plan: Check symmetry by verifying each pair's reverse is in R; check antisymmetry by finding if any (a,b),(b,a) pair has a != b.

steps.title: Test symmetry
steps.formula_raw: Symmetric: for all a,b: (a,b) in R -> (b,a) in R
steps.apply: Check each pair in R: (x,y) in R -> is (y,x) in R? YES. (y,x) in R -> is (x,y) in R? YES. (z,z) in R -> is (z,z) in R? YES. All pairs in R have their reverse in R, so R IS symmetric.
steps.note: R is symmetric because every pair in R has its mirror pair also in R.

steps.title: Test antisymmetry
steps.formula_raw: Antisymmetric: for all a,b: (a,b) in R AND (b,a) in R -> a = b
steps.apply: We have (x,y) in R AND (y,x) in R, but x != y. This violates antisymmetry. So R is NOT antisymmetric.
steps.note: The pair (x,y)/(y,x) with x != y is the counterexample to antisymmetry.

steps.title: Identify correct option
steps.apply: R is symmetric (from step 1) but NOT antisymmetric (from step 2). This matches option A: 'R is symmetric but NOT antisymmetric.'
steps.note: The mutual pair (x,y)/(y,x) proves both symmetry (reverse pair exists) and non-antisymmetry (two distinct mutually-related elements).

solution.result: R is symmetric (every pair has its reverse in R) but NOT antisymmetric (the mutual pair (x,y)/(y,x) with x != y violates antisymmetry). Answer: A. R is symmetric but NOT antisymmetric.

questions.formula_note: Checking relation properties requires testing each pair in R systematically; the mutual pair (x,y)/(y,x) simultaneously proves symmetry (reverse pair present) and disproves antisymmetry (two distinct mutually-related elements), making A the correct answer.

questions.question:
What is the possible number of reflexive relations on a set of 5 elements?
A. 2^10
B. 2^15
C. 2^20
D. 2^25
questions.to_find: The number of reflexive relations on a 5-element set

understand.plain: A relation on an n-element set is a subset of the n^2 ordered pairs. A reflexive relation must include all n diagonal pairs (a,a). The remaining n^2 - n off-diagonal pairs can be freely included or excluded. So there are 2^(n^2 - n) reflexive relations. For n=5: 2^(25-5) = 2^20.

keywords.explain: A relation R where (a,a) in R for every element a in the set. All diagonal pairs are mandatory.
keywords.example: On {1,2}: any reflexive relation must include (1,1) and (2,2). The 2 off-diagonal pairs (1,2) and (2,1) are optional, giving 2^2 = 4 reflexive relations.

keywords.explain: For an n-element set, there are n^2 - n off-diagonal pairs that are free (not constrained by reflexivity). Each can independently be in or out of the relation.
keywords.example: For n=5: 25 - 5 = 20 free pairs, giving 2^20 choices.

style='fill:#fff'>1</text><text x='54' y='50' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>1</text><text x='94' y='80' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>1</text><text x='134' y='110' class='tag' x='225' y='118' class='sub mut'>5 fixed</text><text x='225' y='136' class='sub mut'>Off-diag:</text><text x='225' y='154' class='sub mut'>20 free</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 5x5 matrix where the 5 diagonal cells are shaded green (must be 1 for reflexivity) and the 20 off-diagonal cells are free (0 or 1), giving 2^20 reflexive relations.

given.aim: Count all possible reflexive relations on a set of 5 elements.

terms.meaning: The number of elements in the set; here n = 5
terms.connects: Total ordered pairs = n^2 = 25

terms.meaning: The n pairs of the form (a,a); these MUST be in any reflexive relation
terms.connects: Fixing all diagonal pairs ensures reflexivity

terms.meaning: The n^2 - n = 20 pairs (a,b) with a != b; each is freely chosen
terms.connects: 2^20 ways to set these gives the count of reflexive relations

given.plan: Fix all n diagonal pairs (required for reflexivity). The remaining n^2 - n off-diagonal pairs are unconstrained, giving 2^(n^2 - n) reflexive relations total.

steps.title: Count total and fixed pairs
steps.formula_raw: Number of reflexive relations on an n-element set = 2^(n^2 - n)
steps.apply: For n = 5: Total pairs = n^2 = 25. Diagonal (fixed) pairs = n = 5. Free off-diagonal pairs = 25 - 5 = 20.
steps.note: Each of the 20 off-diagonal pairs independently can be included or not.

steps.title: Apply counting formula
steps.apply: Number of reflexive relations = 2^20. This matches option C.
steps.note: 2^20 = 1,048,576, a large number reflecting the freedom in choosing off-diagonal entries.

questions.formula_note: The key formula is 2^(n^2 - n): reflexivity mandates all n diagonal pairs, and the remaining n^2 - n off-diagonal pairs are free, each contributing an independent binary choice.

questions.question:
Let R be the relation on the set of positive integers such that aRb if and only if a and b are distinct and have a common divisor other than 1. Which one of the following statements about R is true?
A. R is symmetric and reflexive but not transitive
B. R is reflexive but not symmetric and not transitive
C. R is transitive but not symmetric
D. R is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive
questions.answer: D. R is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive
questions.to_find: Properties (reflexive, symmetric, transitive) of the 'share a common divisor > 1' relation on positive integers

understand.plain: Two distinct positive integers are related if they share a common factor greater than 1 (i.e., gcd > 1). Reflexivity fails because a is not distinct from itself. Symmetry holds because gcd is symmetric. Transitivity fails: for example 2 and 6 share factor 2, and 6 and 9 share factor 3, but 2 and 9 share no common factor > 1.

keywords.explain: aRb requires a and b to be DISTINCT, so aRa never holds. R is not reflexive.
keywords.example: 1 R 1 fails because 1 and 1 are not distinct (and gcd(1,1)=1 anyway).

keywords.explain: If gcd(a,b) > 1 then gcd(b,a) > 1 and both are distinct iff the other is distinct. So aRb implies bRa.
keywords.example: 6 R 4 (gcd=2, distinct) and 4 R 6 (gcd=2, distinct): symmetric.

keywords.explain: aRb and bRc does NOT imply aRc. The element b can share different prime factors with a and c.
keywords.example: 2 R 6 (gcd=2) and 6 R 9 (gcd=3), but gcd(2,9)=1 so 2 NOT R 9.

understand.visual_alt: The 'common factor > 1' relation is symmetric (gcd is symmetric) but fails reflexivity (distinctness required) and fails transitivity (2-6-9 is a counterexample).

given.aim: Determine which combination of reflexive/symmetric/transitive holds for R on positive integers, where aRb iff a != b and gcd(a,b) > 1.

terms.meaning: aRb iff a != b and gcd(a,b) > 1 (they share a prime factor)
terms.connects: Being distinct and sharing a prime divisor defines the relation

terms.meaning: Greatest common divisor of a and b
terms.connects: R holds iff gcd > 1 AND a != b

terms.meaning: Three integers where middle shares a prime with each end but the ends share no prime
terms.connects: This chain proves R is not transitive

given.plan: Check reflexivity (does aRa hold?), symmetry (does aRb imply bRa?), and transitivity (does aRb, bRc imply aRc?) by definitions and counterexamples.

steps.title: Test reflexivity
steps.formula_raw: Reflexive: for all a, (a,a) in R
steps.apply: For aRa to hold, a must be distinct from a, which is never true. So aRa NEVER holds for any positive integer a. R is NOT reflexive.
steps.note: The distinctness requirement in the definition immediately rules out reflexivity.

steps.title: Test symmetry
steps.apply: If aRb then a != b and gcd(a,b) > 1. Since gcd(a,b) = gcd(b,a) and b != a, we have bRa. So R IS symmetric.
steps.note: gcd is commutative, so the property transfers automatically.

steps.title: Test transitivity with counterexample
steps.formula_raw: Transitive: (a,b) in R AND (b,c) in R -> (a,c) in R
steps.apply: Take a=2, b=6, c=9. gcd(2,6)=2>1 and 2!=6, so 2R6. gcd(6,9)=3>1 and 6!=9, so 6R9. But gcd(2,9)=1, so 2 NOT R 9. Transitivity FAILS.
steps.note: The integer 6 = 2*3 acts as a 'bridge' sharing different prime factors with 2 and 9.

solution.result: R is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive. Answer: D. R is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that while gcd symmetry guarantees R is symmetric, the distinctness requirement kills reflexivity and the bridge-prime counterexample (2-6-9) kills transitivity.

questions.question:
Let R be a relation on the set of ordered pairs of positive integers such that ((p,q),(r,s)) is in R if and only if p - q = s - r. Which one of the following is true about R?
A. Both reflexive and symmetric
B. Reflexive but not symmetric
C. Not reflexive but symmetric
D. Neither reflexive nor symmetric
questions.to_find: Whether the relation R defined by p - q = s - r on ordered pairs of positive integers is reflexive and/or symmetric

understand.plain: The condition p - q = s - r can be rewritten as p + r = q + s (equivalently p - q = -(r - s)). For reflexivity, (p,q) R (p,q) requires p - q = q - p, which means p = q. Since p and q can be any positive integers, this fails when p != q. For symmetry, if p - q = s - r then s - r = p - q, which by rewriting gives r - s = q - p, i.e., r - s = -(p - q) and s - r = p - q: symmetric holds.

keywords.explain: Equivalently p + r = q + s or p - q = -(r - s). Two ordered pairs are related when the 'signed difference' of the first equals the negation of the signed difference of the second.
(p,q) R (p,q) requires p - q = q - p, so 2(p-q) = 0, so p = q. Fails for any p != q.
If ((p,q),(r,s)) in R then p-q = s-r. For the reverse: ((r,s),(p,q)) in R requires r-s = q-p = -(p-q) = -(s-r) = r-s. Yes, r-s = r-s is trivially true from p-q = s-r.
keywords.example: (3,1) R (2,4) and also (2,4) R (3,1): 2-4=-2, 1-3=-2. Symmetric confirmed.

understand.visual_alt: The relation groups ordered pairs by the value of their component sum (p+r = q+s): symmetric pairs are related but no pair is related to itself unless both components are equal.

given.aim: Test reflexivity and symmetry of R on ordered pairs of positive integers where ((p,q),(r,s)) in R iff p - q = s - r.

terms.meaning: Ordered pairs of positive integers, elements of N x N
terms.example: (3,1) and (2,4) are ordered pairs of positive integers
terms.connects: The condition relates pairs from N x N to each other

terms.meaning: The defining condition; equivalent to p + r = q + s
terms.connects: This is NOT the same as p-q = r-s (which would give an equivalence relation)

terms.meaning: Setting (r,s) = (p,q) gives p - q = q - p, requiring p = q
terms.connects: Not all pairs are self-related, so not reflexive

given.plan: Substitute (r,s) = (p,q) to test reflexivity; swap to test symmetry; give examples.

steps.title: Test reflexivity
steps.formula_raw: Reflexive: for all (p,q), ((p,q),(p,q)) in R, i.e., p - q = q - p
steps.apply: p - q = q - p iff 2(p - q) = 0 iff p = q. For p != q (e.g., p=2, q=1): 2 - 1 = 1 but q - p = 1 - 2 = -1, and 1 != -1. So ((2,1),(2,1)) NOT in R. R is NOT reflexive.
steps.note: Only the diagonal ordered pairs (p,p) are self-related; all others are not.

steps.title: Test symmetry
steps.apply: Assume p - q = s - r. We need to verify r - s = q - p for the reverse pair. From p - q = s - r, multiply both sides by -1: q - p = r - s. So r - s = q - p, which is exactly the condition for ((r,s),(p,q)) in R. R IS symmetric.
steps.note: The condition p - q = s - r is self-dual under swapping (p,q) and (r,s).

solution.result: R is not reflexive but is symmetric. Answer: C. Not reflexive but symmetric.

questions.formula_note: The condition p - q = s - r (equivalently p + r = q + s) is antisymmetric in the signed-differences sense, causing reflexivity to fail for unequal-component pairs while preserving symmetry by sign-flip equivalence.

questions.question:
A binary relation R on N x N is defined as follows: (a,b) R (c,d) if a <= c or b <= d. Consider the following propositions:
P: R is reflexive.
Q: R is transitive.
Which one of the following statements is TRUE?
A. Both P and Q are true.
B. P is true and Q is false.
C. P is false and Q is true.
D. Both P and Q are false.
questions.answer: B. P is true and Q is false.
questions.to_find: Whether R on N x N defined by (a,b)R(c,d) iff a <= c or b <= d is reflexive and/or transitive

understand.plain: The relation holds if at least one of the two components satisfies the less-than-or-equal condition. Reflexivity holds trivially since a <= a is always true (so the first condition a <= c is satisfied when c = a). Transitivity fails: there exist triples where the first two pairs are related and the last two pairs are related but the first and last are not.

keywords.explain: For any (a,b) in N x N, (a,b) R (a,b) iff a <= a or b <= b. Both a <= a and b <= b are true (reflexivity of <=), so R is always reflexive.
Need (a,b)R(c,d) and (c,d)R(e,f) but NOT (a,b)R(e,f). The 'or' condition makes this possible when the component satisfying the condition switches between steps.
keywords.example: (3,1) R (2,2): 3<=2? No, but 1<=2? Yes. (2,2) R (1,3): 2<=1? No, but 2<=3? Yes. Now (3,1) R (1,3): 3<=1? No. 1<=3? Yes. So (3,1) R (1,3) IS true. Need a better counterexample.

understand.visual_alt: The relation (a,b)R(c,d) iff a<=c OR b<=d is reflexive (trivially, since a<=a) but fails transitivity: e.g., (3,1)R(1,2) via b-component and (1,2)R(2,0) is... need careful counterexample.

given.aim: Determine if R on N x N with (a,b)R(c,d) iff a <= c or b <= d is reflexive and/or transitive.

terms.meaning: The set of all ordered pairs of natural numbers
terms.example: (1,2), (3,5), (0,0) are elements of N x N
terms.connects: R relates pairs from this set to each other

terms.term: Condition a <= c or b <= d
terms.meaning: The relation holds if at least one component of the first pair is <= the corresponding component of the second pair
terms.connects: Disjunctive condition makes the relation 'weak' and easy to satisfy

terms.meaning: Three pairs where the middle pair relates to both ends (via different components) but the ends are not related
terms.example: (2,1) R (1,2) via b: 1<=2. (1,2) R (2,1) via a: 1<=2. Now (2,1) R (2,1)? Yes (reflexive), so this doesn't work. Try (3,1) R (2,4): 1<=4 yes. (2,4) R (5,2): 2<=5 yes. (3,1) R (5,2): 3<=5 yes. Try harder: (3,1) R (1,3): 1<=3 yes. (1,3) R (2,1): 1<=2 yes. (3,1) R (2,1): 3<=2? No. 1<=1? Yes. Related. Need (a,b) NOT related to (e,f): need a>e AND b>f. So try (3,2) R (1,3): 2<=3 yes. (1,3) R (2,1): 1<=2 yes. (3,2) R (2,1): 3<=2? No. 2<=1? No. NOT related! Counterexample found.
terms.connects: This proves Q (transitivity) is FALSE

given.plan: Check reflexivity by substituting (c,d) = (a,b). Test transitivity by finding a counterexample where the middle element connects via different components.

steps.title: Verify reflexivity (P)
steps.formula_raw: Reflexive: for all (a,b), (a,b) R (a,b), i.e., a <= a or b <= b
steps.apply: For any (a,b) in N x N: a <= a is always true (by reflexivity of <=). So the condition 'a <= a OR b <= b' is always satisfied. P is TRUE: R is reflexive.
steps.note: Even one true disjunct suffices; a <= a always gives truth.

steps.title: Find counterexample to transitivity (Q)
steps.apply: Take (a,b)=(3,2), (c,d)=(1,3), (e,f)=(2,1). Check (3,2)R(1,3): 3<=1? No. 2<=3? Yes. So (3,2)R(1,3). Check (1,3)R(2,1): 1<=2? Yes. So (1,3)R(2,1). Check (3,2)R(2,1): 3<=2? No. 2<=1? No. NOT related! Transitivity FAILS. Q is FALSE.
steps.note: The bridge element (1,3) satisfies the b-component condition with (3,2) and the a-component condition with (2,1), but these don't combine to relate (3,2) to (2,1).

solution.result: P (reflexive) is TRUE, Q (transitive) is FALSE. Answer: B. P is true and Q is false.

questions.formula_note: The 'or' condition makes R reflexive trivially (a<=a always) but breaks transitivity because the satisfying component can switch between steps, leaving no single component consistently ordered end-to-end.

formulas.name: Number of Reflexive Relations on an n-Element Set
formulas.whenToUse: Counting how many distinct reflexive relations can be defined on a set with n elements

terms.means: number of elements in the set
total number of ordered pairs in the full Cartesian product
number of off-diagonal pairs free to be included or excluded

formulas.trap: Don't confuse with the total number of relations (2^(n^2)) or the number of symmetric relations (2^(n(n+1)/2)). Only diagonal pairs are fixed; all off-diagonal pairs are free.
formulas.reference: Standard combinatorics of binary relations on finite sets
